title: STANDARD FOR OKRA CXS 318-2014 Adopted in 2014.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR OKRA CXS 318-2014 Adopted in 2014.
sections.summary: Document title: Standard for Okra (CXS 318-2014), adopted in 2014 (heading only).

Defines the scope as commercial varieties of okra (Abelmoschus esculentus) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging, and explicitly excludes okra intended for industrial processing.

Sets quality provisions and classification: okra must be whole with a clean‑cut peduncle, fresh, clean, practically free of pests and foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture, off‑odours/tastes and temperature damage, and able to withstand transport; maturity must be sufficient (not fibrous), and okra is classified into Extra, Class I and Class II with specified allowable slight defects and tolerances for appearance and skin defects.

Lists minimum quality requirements applicable to all classes: whole with clean‑cut peduncle, fresh and sound (no rotting), clean and practically free of foreign matter and pests, free of abnormal moisture, foreign smell/taste and temperature damage, and fit to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.
Requires okra to be sufficiently developed and not fibrous (maturity requirement).

Classifies okra into three classes: 'Extra' (superior quality, essentially defect‑free), Class I (good quality with slight permissible defects such as up to 5% surface area discolouration and up to 2% skin defects), and Class II (meets minimum requirements with greater allowable defects, e.g. up to 10% discolouration and up to 5% skin defects).

Specifies the 'Extra' class for okra as superior quality: pods must be firm, characteristic of the variety in shape/appearance/development, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Defines Class I okra as good quality and allows slight defects provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation, including slight shape/development defects, colouring defects up to 5% of total surface area, and skin defects (scars, blemishes, scratches, bruises) up to 2% of total surface area.

Class II covers okra that does not qualify for higher classes but meets the minimum requirements in Section 2.1 and may have defects (shape/development, colouring up to 10% of surface, skin defects up to 5% of surface) provided the okra retains its essential characteristics regarding quality, keeping quality and presentation.
Size is determined by okra length in cm (without peduncle) and an optional guide table provides size codes 1–5 corresponding to lengths: 2.0–4.0, >4.0–6.0, >6.0–8.0, >8.0–10.0 and >10 cm.

Allows tolerances for packages containing okra that do not meet the declared class: quality tolerances (Extra: 5% by number/weight not meeting class but meeting Class I or its tolerances; Class I: 10% meeting Class II or its tolerances; Class II: 10% not meeting class or minimum requirements except produce unfit for consumption) and size tolerances (10% by number may be size immediately above or below); also sets presentation requirements on uniformity and packaging (materials new/clean/food-grade, compliance with CXC 44‑1995 and containers meeting quality/hygiene/ventilation/resistance, free of foreign matter and smell).

Specifies quality tolerances for okra packages: Extra class allows 5% by number/weight not meeting the class but meeting Class I (or its tolerances), Class I allows 10% not meeting class but meeting Class II (or its tolerances), Class II allows 10% failing class and minimum requirements except produce unfit for consumption, and size tolerances for all classes permit 10% by number corresponding to the adjacent size.
Permits up to 5% by number or weight of okra in the 'Extra' class that do not meet Extra requirements but meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Permits up to 10% by number or weight of okra in Class I that do not meet Class I requirements but meet Class II or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

Allows up to 10% by number or weight of okra in Class II that satisfy neither the class nor the minimum requirements, except for produce affected by rotting, marked bruising or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption.
For all classes, allows 10% by number of okra in a package to correspond to the size immediately above or below the size indicated on the package.

Sets presentation requirements: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size with the visible portion representative) and packaging must protect produce using new/clean/food‑grade materials with non‑toxic printing/adhesives, comply with CXC 44‑1995, and containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Requires package contents to be uniform—okra of the same origin, variety or commercial type, quality and size—with the visible portion representative of the entire contents.

Requires okra to be packed to protect the produce, using materials inside the package that are new, clean and of food‑grade quality (recycled food‑grade allowed), permits paper/stamps bearing trade specifications if printed with non‑toxic ink/glue, and mandates packing in compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995).
Containers must provide appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance for handling, shipping and preserving okra, and packages must be free of foreign matter and smell.

Labelling requirements supplement the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 119‑1985): consumer packages must meet specific provisions (e.g., nature of produce if not visible) and non‑retail containers must bear legible indelible particulars or accompanying documents including identification, nature of produce, origin, commercial identification (class and size) and an optional official inspection mark.
States that consumer package labelling must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 119‑1985) and that additional specific provisions follow.
Requires that if okra is not visible from outside the consumer package, the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may indicate the variety and/or commercial type.

Non‑retail containers must bear, legibly and indelibly on the same side or in accompanying documents, particulars including identification, nature of produce, origin, commercial identification (class and size) and may display an official inspection mark.
Requires non‑retail packages to show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher and allows an optional identification code.
Specifies that the nature of produce on non‑retail packages must state 'okra' if contents are not visible and may optionally name the variety and/or commercial type.
Label must indicate the country of origin and may optionally include the district where grown or a national, regional or local place name.
Labeling shall include class and size (either a size code or minimum and maximum length in cm), and if the size code differs from the Table it should be labeled accordingly.
Section heading only indicating an optional official inspection mark; no substantive provisions provided.
The produce shall comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Preparation and handling are recommended to follow Codex hygiene texts (CXC 1-1969, CXC 53-2003 and other relevant codes) and the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997; national legislation may require declaration of name and address, but where a code mark is used the reference 'packer and/or dispatcher (or equivalent abbreviations)' must be indicated close to the code mark.
